hero.tagline: The math and code every robotics book assumes you already know
hero.intro: Most robotics tutorials drop you into matrix transforms and Bayesian filters without warning. This branch is the prereq pack so that doesn't happen to you. Linear algebra (poses + frames are just matrices), calculus and optimization (planners minimize cost), probability (sensors are noisy), Python and C++ idioms (numpy and Eigen show up everywhere), debugging patterns, and curated books. None of it is robotics-specific yet - that's the point. Skip what you already know.

sections.heading: Why these foundations actually matter
sections.body:
Robotics looks like an engineering discipline (it builds machines) but it runs on the math of physics, statistics, and optimization. You can copy ROS 2 tutorials all day and the moment a TF tree breaks, an EKF diverges, or your IK solver oscillates, you'll need the underlying math to even understand what's wrong. Same for code - you'll spend more time staring at numpy broadcasting bugs and Eigen template errors than at robot kinematics.

The good news: the math is finite. Solid linear algebra + calculus + probability covers 90% of what shows up. The remaining 10% (Lie groups, optimization on manifolds, info theory) you pick up as needed.
bullets: Linear algebra → kinematics, control, perception, every ML model
Calculus + optimization → planning, MPC, RL, Newton-style IK solvers
Probability + statistics → Kalman / particle filters, sensor fusion, inference
Numerical methods → IK iterations, ODE integration, control loop stability
ROS 2 patterns + debugging → 50% of your time in any real project
Knowing where to look → papers with code, ROS Answers, GitHub issues, Discords

sections.heading: Linear algebra - the language of poses
sections.body:
Every pose, every transform, every control law is a matrix expression. The non-negotiable basics: matrix multiplication and its meaning as composed transforms, dot/cross product, transpose, inverse, eigenvalues + eigenvectors, SVD, rank and null space. The robotics-specific extensions: rotation matrices SO(3), homogeneous transforms SE(3), the Lie algebra so(3) (skew-symmetric matrices that exponentiate into rotations), and the Jacobian (the gradient of an end-effector pose with respect to joint angles).

You'll write almost none of this from scratch - numpy and Eigen do the heavy lifting. But you absolutely need the intuition for what a singular Jacobian means (the IK solver is about to explode), what a non-orthonormal rotation matrix means (you've drifted; re-orthonormalize), and what 'in the null space of J' means (free motion that doesn't move the end-effector). Drag the vectors in the widget below to feel dot products and rotations.
bullets: Matrix multiplication = composition of linear transforms; reading order is right-to-left
Eigenvalues of a covariance matrix = principal axes of uncertainty
SVD = swiss-army knife for least squares, pseudoinverse, low-rank approximation, ICP
Rotations: 3×3 matrix or quaternion or axis-angle - same underlying SO(3) group
Jacobian J: small joint changes → end-effector velocity. det(J) → 0 = singularity
Lie groups (SO(3), SE(3)) - manifold-valued state needs special integration / interpolation

codeBlocks.title: The 5 numpy operations you'll write 1000 times
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]])
b = np.array([5, 6])

A @ b              # matrix-vector multiply (NOT A * b - that's elementwise!)
A.T                # transpose
np.linalg.inv(A)   # full inverse - avoid; prefer solve
np.linalg.solve(A, b)              # Ax = b without explicit inverse
np.linalg.pinv(A)                  # pseudoinverse for rectangular A
u, s, vt = np.linalg.svd(A)        # SVD

codeBlocks.title: Damped pseudoinverse - the IK workhorse

sections.heading: Calculus + optimization
sections.body:
Calculus shows up everywhere a robot makes a continuous decision. Derivatives describe how outputs respond to inputs (Jacobians are matrices of partial derivatives). Integrals turn velocity → position, acceleration → velocity (every controller integrates somewhere). Optimization - minimizing a cost subject to constraints - is the unifying frame for path planning, MPC, learning-from-demonstrations, and basically every modern decision algorithm.

You don't need real analysis. You need: chain rule, gradient + gradient descent, the Hessian and Newton's method, Lagrange multipliers (constrained optimization), and convexity - knowing when a problem is convex (one global minimum) vs non-convex (you're stuck with local minima).
bullets: Chain rule = the engine of every backpropagation pass and Jacobian computation
Gradient descent: x ← x − α∇f(x). Almost everything in ML is a variant
Newton's method: x ← x − H⁻¹∇f. Converges fast when H exists; expensive in high-dim
Convex vs non-convex: convex means QP/SOCP solvers find the global minimum
Lagrange multipliers: handle equality constraints; KKT for inequalities
Numerical integration: Euler (1st order, fast, drifts) → RK4 (4th order, slower, accurate)

codeBlocks.title: Gradient descent for a tiny least-squares problem
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np

A = np.random.randn(50, 5)
b = A @ np.array([1.0, 2, 3, 4, 5]) + 0.1 * np.random.randn(50)

x = np.zeros(5)
for _ in range(2000):
    grad = 2 * A.T @ (A @ x - b)         # ∇ ||Ax - b||²
    x -= 0.005 * grad
print(x)                                  # ≈ [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
# Closed form (sanity check): np.linalg.lstsq(A, b)[0]

codeBlocks.title: Convex QP via cvxpy - basis of MPC
codeBlocks.code:
import cvxpy as cp
import numpy as np

x = cp.Variable(3)
Q = np.eye(3); c = np.array([1, -2, 0.5])
prob = cp.Problem(cp.Minimize(0.5 * cp.quad_form(x, Q) + c @ x),
                  [x >= -1, x <= 1, cp.sum(x) == 0.5])
prob.solve()
print(x.value)

sections.heading: Probability + statistics
sections.body:
Robots are built on noisy sensors and uncertain models, so robotics is fundamentally probabilistic. You need: Bayes' theorem (the prior + likelihood → posterior recipe behind every state estimator), Gaussians (mean + covariance and how they propagate through linear transforms), maximum likelihood (the cost function behind every fit), and the central limit theorem (why 'noise looks Gaussian' is usually true enough).

The prereqs unlock entire robotics subfields: Kalman filters become 'Bayes' rule for Gaussians', particle filters become 'Bayes' rule for arbitrary distributions', SLAM becomes 'maximum a posteriori over a pose graph', RL becomes 'maximize expected reward'.
bullets: P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A) / P(B) - Bayes' theorem is the unifying equation of state estimation
Gaussians: closed under linear transforms; product of Gaussians is Gaussian
Covariance matrices: eigenvalues = uncertainty along principal axes
MLE = maximize likelihood under data; equivalent to minimizing negative log-likelihood
MAP = MLE + prior. RL is MAP with reward as log-likelihood
Markov assumption: future depends only on present, not history. Drives MDPs, HMMs, KFs

codeBlocks.title: Multivariate Gaussian propagation - the core of any KF
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np

# Prior: x ~ N(mu, Sigma)
mu    = np.array([0.0, 0.0])
Sigma = np.array([[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 0.5]])

# Linear transform: y = A x + N(0, Q)
A = np.array([[1.0, 0.1], [0.0, 1.0]])
Q = 0.01 * np.eye(2)

mu_y    = A @ mu                      # mean propagates as A·mu
Sigma_y = A @ Sigma @ A.T + Q         # cov propagates as A·Σ·Aᵀ + Q
# That's literally the Kalman predict step.

codeBlocks.title: Bayes update - posterior from prior + likelihood (1D)
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np

def gauss_product(mu1, var1, mu2, var2):
    """Posterior mean + variance from two independent Gaussian beliefs."""
    var = 1 / (1 / var1 + 1 / var2)
    mu  = var * (mu1 / var1 + mu2 / var2)
    return mu, var

# Sensor measurement update: belief = prior * likelihood / norm
prior_mu, prior_var = 5.0, 2.0
meas_mu,  meas_var  = 6.5, 1.0
posterior_mu, posterior_var = gauss_product(prior_mu, prior_var, meas_mu, meas_var)
# posterior_mu ≈ 6.0  (pulled toward the lower-variance measurement)

links.label: Sebastian Thrun - Udacity AI for Robotics

sections.heading: Python for robotics - the patterns you'll use daily
sections.body: Python is the lingua franca of robotics ML and prototyping. The robotics-specific muscle memory: numpy for linear algebra, scipy.spatial.transform for rotations, scipy.optimize for solvers, matplotlib for quick plots, and rclpy for ROS 2 nodes. The common gotchas - broadcasting rules, in-place vs out-of-place ops, GIL constraints for real-time, and the cost of pure-Python loops on big tensors - are the things that separate slow code from fast code.
bullets: Vectorize: replace for-loops with numpy ops. 100× speedup is normal
Broadcasting rules: align trailing dimensions, missing dims = size 1
Avoid np.linalg.inv(A) - use np.linalg.solve(A, b) instead (faster + more accurate)
scipy.spatial.transform.Rotation handles every conversion you'll need
asyncio + rclpy: async-friendly node structures
GIL: pure-Python parallelism is fake. Use multiprocessing or NumPy under the hood for real parallelism

codeBlocks.title: Idiomatic ROS 2 Python node template
codeBlocks.code:
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist
from sensor_msgs.msg import LaserScan

class MyNode(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('my_node')
        self.declare_parameter('rate', 10.0)
        rate = self.get_parameter('rate').value

        self.sub = self.create_subscription(LaserScan, '/scan', self.on_scan, 10)
        self.pub = self.create_publisher(Twist, '/cmd_vel', 10)
        self.create_timer(1.0 / rate, self.on_tick)
        self.last_min_range = float('inf')

    def on_scan(self, msg: LaserScan):
        self.last_min_range = float(min(msg.ranges))

    def on_tick(self):
        cmd = Twist()
        cmd.linear.x = 0.2 if self.last_min_range > 0.5 else 0.0
        self.pub.publish(cmd)

def main():
    rclpy.init(); rclpy.spin(MyNode())

codeBlocks.title: Vectorize before optimizing - the perception speedup pattern

sections.heading: C++ for robotics - modern idioms
sections.body:
Real-time robot control runs in C++ for predictable timing. Modern C++ (C++17 / 20) is far less painful than the C++98 you might have learned: smart pointers handle memory, range-for loops are clean, std::variant + std::optional remove most pointer-juggling, structured bindings make APIs readable. For robotics specifically: Eigen for linear algebra, Boost.PFR / cereal for serialization, fmt for logging.

The killer feature for real-time: deterministic memory. Allocate everything upfront, use object pools or std::array, never call malloc / new in the hot loop. ROS 2 + ros2_control follow this convention.
bullets: std::shared_ptr / std::unique_ptr: never new / delete by hand again
Eigen: header-only linear algebra, expression-template magic for fast math
auto, range-for, structured bindings - modern C++ reads like Python now
constexpr + concepts (C++20): catch bugs at compile time, not runtime
Real-time hot loop: no malloc, no exceptions, no virtual calls. Pre-allocate everything
rclcpp pubs/subs use shared_ptr-by-default; understand the IPC vs serialization trade

codeBlocks.title: Modern ROS 2 C++ node template
Eigen - linear algebra without misery

sections.heading: Debugging robots - patterns that save your weekend
sections.body: Half of robotics is debugging. Production teams have a vocabulary of repeating failure modes: 'tf is wrong' (mismatched frames), 'topic frequency dropped' (publisher died), 'silent diverged' (NaN crept in), 'works in sim, fails on hardware' (timing or signal noise). The fix-loop is always the same: reproduce in a recording, narrow to the smallest case, instrument, fix, regression-test by replaying the bag.
bullets: First step every time: ros2 topic hz <topic> - is the topic alive at the expected rate?
ros2 topic echo --once - print one message; does it look right?
ros2 run tf2_tools view_frames - generates frames.pdf showing the live TF tree
ros2 bag record everything when something weird happens - replay it later
Foxglove Studio = the modern bag visualizer; faster than RViz for forensic analysis
GDB/lldb for C++ crashes; py-spy for Python performance; perf for kernel-level profiling

codeBlocks.title: 5-command first-aid for any ROS 2 node
codeBlocks.code:
# 1. Is the node alive?
ros2 node list | grep my_node

# 2. What does it publish/subscribe?
ros2 node info /my_node

# 3. Is its main topic publishing?
ros2 topic hz /my_node/output

# 4. What does the message actually look like?
ros2 topic echo /my_node/output --once

# 5. Is TF healthy?
ros2 run tf2_tools view_frames
xdg-open frames.pdf

codeBlocks.title: Catch a NaN before it propagates
codeBlocks.code:
# In Python
import numpy as np
assert np.all(np.isfinite(state)), f'NaN in state: {state}'

# In C++ with Eigen
assert(state.allFinite() && "NaN in state");

sections.heading: Robotics math cookbook - the formulas you'll actually use
sections.body: Every robotics textbook scatters its key formulas across chapters. This is the one-page lookup you'll reach for in real work: composing transforms, converting between rotation representations, integrating angular velocity, computing twists. Bookmark it.
bullets: Homogeneous transform: T = [[R, t], [0, 1]] - 4x4 matrix encoding rotation R + translation t
Compose transforms: T_AC = T_AB · T_BC (apply right-to-left in row-vector form)
Invert a transform: T_BA = [[R^T, -R^T·t], [0, 1]] - O(1) close-form, no matrix inverse needed
Quaternion to rotation matrix: R = I + 2w·[v]× + 2[v]×² where v = (x,y,z), [·]× is the skew-symmetric form
Quaternion compose: q1·q2 (Hamilton product). NEVER add quaternions
Quaternion slerp: smooth interpolation along the great-circle on S^3, used for trajectory orientation
Axis-angle to quaternion: q = (sin(θ/2)·ω̂, cos(θ/2)) where ω̂ is the unit axis
Rodrigues' formula: R = I + sin(θ)·[ω̂]× + (1-cos(θ))·[ω̂]× ² - rotation matrix from axis-angle
Twist (body velocity): V = (ω, v) - angular + linear velocity. Lives in se(3)
Adjoint: maps a twist between frames. Ad_T · V_A = V_B for relative motion
Integrate angular velocity: q(t+dt) = q(t) ⊗ exp(½·dt·ω) (use quaternion exp - keeps unit norm)
Skew-symmetric matrix: [v]× turns cross product into matrix multiply. v × u = [v]× · u
Pose error: log(T_target^-1 · T_current) - a 6-vector twist suitable for any controller

codeBlocks.title: scipy + numpy: every conversion you'll ever do
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np
from scipy.spatial.transform import Rotation as R, Slerp

# Quaternion (xyzw) → rotation matrix
q = [0, 0, np.sin(np.pi/4), np.cos(np.pi/4)]   # 90° about z
rot = R.from_quat(q)
M = rot.as_matrix()             # 3x3

# Compose two rotations
q1 = R.from_euler('z', 90, degrees=True)
q2 = R.from_euler('y', 30, degrees=True)
q12 = q1 * q2                   # apply q2 then q1

# Slerp between two orientations
key_times = [0, 1]
key_rots  = R.from_quat([[0,0,0,1], [0,0,np.sin(np.pi/4),np.cos(np.pi/4)]])
slerp = Slerp(key_times, key_rots)
q_at_05 = slerp([0.5]).as_quat()

# Homogeneous transform compose / invert
def T_from(R_, t):
    T = np.eye(4); T[:3,:3] = R_; T[:3,3] = t; return T
def T_inv(T):
    R_ = T[:3,:3]; t = T[:3,3]
    Ti = np.eye(4); Ti[:3,:3] = R_.T; Ti[:3,3] = -R_.T @ t; return Ti

T_AB = T_from(M, [1, 0, 0])
T_BA = T_inv(T_AB)

# Skew-symmetric
def skew(v):
    x, y, z = v
    return np.array([[0,-z, y],[ z, 0,-x],[-y, x, 0]])

# Rodrigues: axis-angle → rotation matrix
def rodrigues(axis, theta):
    a = axis / np.linalg.norm(axis)
    K = skew(a)
    return np.eye(3) + np.sin(theta)*K + (1-np.cos(theta))*(K @ K)

codeBlocks.title: Eigen / Sophus equivalents in C++

sections.heading: Books, courses, papers - what to read
sections.body: Curated, opinionated, no filler. Pick one book per area and finish it before moving on. Open courseware - MIT OCW, Stanford SOE, Coursera specializations - works if you commit. For modern ML / VLA papers, follow Papers With Code and the Hugging Face blog; the field moves fast.
bullets: Modern Robotics (Lynch, Park) - kinematics + dynamics + planning. FREE PDF
Probabilistic Robotics (Thrun, Burgard, Fox) - every state estimator, definitive
Robotics Modelling, Planning and Control (Siciliano) - the European canon
Convex Optimization (Boyd, Vandenberghe) - FREE; the optimization bible
Sutton & Barto - Reinforcement Learning. FREE; the RL canon
Deep Learning (Goodfellow) - the ML side
Programming Robots with ROS (Quigley) - practical ROS 1, mostly transfers to 2
ChatGPT / Claude as a study partner - explain a paper to it, have it quiz you back

sections.heading: Communities - where to ask, where to share
sections.body: Robotics is too broad for any single person to know. Knowing where to ask is a skill. The good places: ROS Discourse (best for serious technical questions), the ROS / Open Robotics Discord, the r/ROS subreddit, GitHub issues on the package you're stuck on, Stack Overflow's `ros2` tag, and X/Twitter / BlueSky for following research directly from authors. For ML-heavy work, the Hugging Face / LeRobot Discord is now the most active community.
bullets: ROS Discourse (discourse.ros.org) - the canonical Q&A; takes ROS 2 questions seriously
ROS / Open Robotics Discord - fastest casual help; mentions get attention
GitHub issues on the package you're stuck on - search closed issues first
Stack Overflow tag: ros2 - declining in volume but still well-indexed by Google
r/ROS, r/robotics - community Q&A, projects, drama
X / BlueSky researchers: @gerkey, @drethage, @sergeylevine, @chelseabfinn, @karpathy
Conferences worth attending: ICRA (May), IROS (Oct), CoRL (Nov), RSS (June), ROSCon (Oct)
LeRobot / Hugging Face - modern hub for VLA + IL communities

sections.heading: Careers & interview prep
sections.body:
The robotics job market splits into a handful of recognizable roles. Robotics Software Engineer (general): C++/Python, ROS 2, perception or planning depth. Perception Engineer: vision + lidar fusion, deep learning, sometimes MLOps. Controls Engineer: state-space, MPC, embedded comfort. ML / Foundation Model Engineer: PyTorch, distributed training, manipulation policies. Systems / Embedded: real-time Linux, drivers, hardware bring-up. Pick the role you want to be in and weight your study to match - generalist Github profiles win fewer offers than focused ones.

Interviews are usually a mix of: a coding screen (LeetCode-easy/medium, often graph/heap), a robotics-specific round (TF math, control intuition, debugging story), a system-design round (architect a perception or planning stack on a whiteboard), and a culture/behavioral. Bring a portfolio of working projects with videos.
bullets: Pick a role: Robotics SWE / Perception / Controls / ML / Embedded - different prep
Coding screen: LeetCode easy-mediums, focus on graphs, heaps, sliding window, BFS/DFS
Robotics deep-dive: 'walk me through Kalman / PID / TF / a Jacobian'
System design: 'design a robot that picks groceries from a shelf'. Show layered thinking
Bring videos: a 30-second clip of your project running beats any GitHub README
Hiring market: top roles at Tesla, Boston Dynamics, Waymo, Anduril, Skydio, Figure, Apptronik, Physical Intelligence, Nvidia (Isaac), Amazon Robotics, John Deere, Skydio, RIVR
Salary band (US, 2026): $130k–$220k base for SWE-level robotics roles; +30-50% TC at top labs

codeBlocks.title: Common interview question - implement a 1D Kalman filter from scratch
